competencies.justification: Understanding India's integrated contribution to the overall field of Social Science and the disciplines that constitute it is the chapter's fundamental organizing purpose. The chapter is titled 'Understanding Social Science' and its opening definitional passage frames Social Science as the systematic, interconnected study of human society; 'Social Science as a Study of Disciplines' establishes that no single field can explain society and that Social Science is a group of related disciplines examined together; and the four dedicated discipline sections plus the closing 'Secondary-Stage Social Science' synthesis present these disciplines 'together presenting a comprehensive understanding of the world.' Throughout, India's own contributions are woven in an integrated manner — the Pañchamahābhūtas and vasudhaiva kuṭumbakam, the Arthaśhāstra, the itihāsa-purāṇa tradition, dharma/artha/rājadharma, Panchayati Raj, and India's economic history. Complete removal of this competency structurally dissolves the chapter's organizing purpose, and because the chapter's primary structural activity explicitly crosses all four sub-disciplines (its purpose is the integrated field itself), Rule 7 permits Weight 3 for this cross-cutting integrative competency; Rule 8's positional test is also satisfied, since the field-and-its-disciplines idea is named in the chapter title and concluding synthesis.

The 'History' section is a dedicated, architecturally distinct named section that develops understanding of explaining the past using different types of sources with specific examples from Indian history. It sets out literary and archaeological sources and then presents, with captioned Indian examples, the four source families — literary (the Sāmaveda manuscript, a palm-leaf Tirukkuṟaḷ), archaeological (a Sindhu-Sarasvatī terracotta figurine, a Viṣhṇu sculpture), epigraphic (a Gupta-period Brahmi inscription, Krishnadeva Raya's Kannada inscription at Hampi), and numismatic (a Samudragupta coin, a Mughal coin of Jahangir) — alongside modern methods such as carbon-14 dating and archaeology. This section could stand alone as a learning unit on how historians use sources, satisfying Rule 5(a) for Weight 2.

'Understanding Society through Time and Traditions' is a dedicated, architecturally distinct named section that develops the growth of new indigenous ideas across India in Philosophy and Social Science — precisely the demand this C-code names. It presents the Pañchamahābhūtas as an early philosophical model of nature as an interconnected system, vasudhaiva kuṭumbakam as an idea of human interdependence, and the Arthaśhāstra attributed to Kauṭilya (composed about 2,300 years ago) as systematic thinking on administration, taxation, and the duties of rulers, arguing that such indigenous inquiry into society long predates modern academic disciplines. This section could stand alone as a learning unit on India's intellectual traditions, satisfying Rule 5(a) for Weight 2.

The 'Geography' section is a dedicated, architecturally distinct named section that develops understanding of the inter-relationship between human beings and their physical environment. It defines Geography as the study of the relationships between human societies and their surroundings, examining how people interact with the natural environment and how places influence human life, viewing the world as a system of interdependencies that integrates spatial and temporal perspectives — for instance, how India's long coastline shaped its global interactions. The section introduces the discipline's tools (maps, globes, atlases, GIS, infographics), and its LET'S EXPLORE box has students map their own village or city on NCERT's School Bhuvan portal. As a section that could stand alone as a learning unit on human-environment relationships, it satisfies Rule 5(a) for Weight 2; Rule 7 restricts Weight 3 away from this sub-discipline.

The 'Political Science' section is a dedicated, architecturally distinct named section that develops understanding of governance and the features and history of democratic government in India. It defines Political Science as the study of how power is distributed, decisions are made, and policies implemented across constitutions, governments, and State institutions, and uses India's Panchayati Raj system as an embodiment of grassroots democracy and local self-governance; it traces India's early political thought (dharma, artha, rājadharma, the Arthaśhāstra) and links these ideas to modern democratic practice. This section could stand alone as a learning unit on governance and democracy, satisfying Rule 5(a) for Weight 2; Rule 7 restricts Weight 3 away from this sub-discipline.

The 'Economics' section is a dedicated, architecturally distinct named section that develops understanding of the key features of the economy that this C-code enumerates — production, distribution, and exchange. It defines Economics as the study of how limited resources are used to meet needs, how goods and services are produced, exchanged, and distributed, and how consumers, producers, and governments make decisions balancing goals such as growth and stability or efficiency and fairness, and it situates these features in India's economic history from a leading pre-colonial trading economy to post-independence development. This section could stand alone as a learning unit on how an economy works, satisfying Rule 5(a) for Weight 2; Rule 7 restricts Weight 3 away from this sub-discipline.
